stem: A 4-month-old infant born at term is brought to the regional emergency department on day 4 of a coryzal illness. Her parents report increased work of breathing overnight and reduced feeding (around half her usual intake) but no apnoeas or cyanotic episodes. Sister had a similar illness 2 weeks ago. She is alert and consolable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 58, SpO2 93% RA, T 37.8°C, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Mild intercostal recession, scattered fine inspiratory crackles bilaterally, occasional expiratory wheeze, alert and pink in air
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed; NPA pending; no recent vaccinations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Discharge home with parent education and a planned GP review the next day
options.rationale: Feeding at half normal intake with SpO2 93 percent and increased work of breathing places her above mild category; she does not meet AU discharge criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start high flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min and transfer to PICU at once
options.rationale: HFNC is indicated when standard oxygen fails or for severe bronchiolitis (apnoea, persistent SpO2 under 90 on low flow, severe recession); her presentation is moderate. (Source: PARIS trial 2018; PREDICT 2021)

options.text: Commence nebulised salbutamol every 4 hours and inhaled corticosteroid twice daily
options.rationale: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids do not reduce admission length, oxygen need, or duration in bronchiolitis under 12 months; AU and PREDICT guidance recommends against routine use. (Source: PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline 2021)

options.text: Admit for observation with minimal handling and oral or nasogastric feeds as tolerated
options.rationale: Moderate bronchiolitis (RR 50 to 70, mild recession, SpO2 90 to 94, reduced feeds) is treated with supportive care and minimal handling; oxygen is added only if SpO2 falls below 90 to 92 per local CPG. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis 2023)

options.text: Order chest x-ray and commence empirical intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin
options.rationale: Routine CXR and empirical antibiotics are not indicated in bronchiolitis without focal signs or septic features. (Source: PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline 2021)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis severity in infants under 12 months drives disposition. Moderate disease - RR around 50 to 70, mild to moderate recession, SpO2 90 to 94 percent, reduced feeding around 50 percent - warrants admission for supportive care with minimal handling and replaced feeds. Bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics and routine imaging do not change outcomes. Escalation to oxygen, then HFNC, then CPAP follows worsening work of breathing, oxygen requirement under 90 to 92 percent, or apnoeas.
key_points: Severity = RR, work of breathing, SpO2, feeding, apnoeas, consciousness.
Moderate disease: admit, minimal handling, NG/IV feeds if oral intake under 50 percent.
Avoid bronchodilators, steroids and routine CXR/antibiotics.
HFNC for severe disease or rescue when standard oxygen fails.
Discharge: SpO2 >= 92 RA, feeding well, no recession, parents confident.
explanation.pearls: In bronchiolitis the most reliable predictor of deterioration is apnoea history under 2 months of age - any reported apnoea should trigger admission regardless of current observations.

stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department at 22:00 with a 2-day history of barking cough and a hoarse voice. In triage he has stridor at rest but settles on his father's lap with no agitation. He is fully immunised, otherwise well, and has had no previous croup admissions.

data_table.Examination: Audible stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, alert and interactive, normal air entry bilaterally, no drooling

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mg and admit for 4 hours of observation
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe croup with marked recession or hypoxia; in moderate croup oral dexamethasone is first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Administer humidified oxygen by face mask and arrange portable lateral neck x-ray
options.rationale: Humidified air does not improve croup; neck imaging delays care and risks airway compromise, and is not required for clinical diagnosis. (Source: PREDICT croup guideline 2020)

options.text: Give oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and observe for 4 hours before discharge
options.rationale: AU first-line for moderate croup is a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (max 12 mg); most children improve within 30 to 60 minutes and can be discharged if stridor at rest resolves. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup 2023)

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin and discharge home with paracetamol and parent education
options.rationale: Croup is a viral illness (parainfluenza); antibiotics have no role and delay symptomatic relief. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Arrange anaesthetic review for inhalational induction and elective intubation
options.rationale: Intubation is required only for impending airway failure (silent chest, exhaustion, severe hypoxia); this child is alert and interactive. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Moderate croup (stridor at rest, mild to moderate recession, alert child) is treated with a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg up to 12 mg, with 4 hours of observation. Nebulised adrenaline is added for severe croup. Inhaled steroids (budesonide) are an alternative but less convenient. Most children improve within 30 to 60 minutes; persistent stridor at rest after observation prompts admission.
key_points: Mild = barking cough no stridor at rest: oral dexamethasone, discharge.
Moderate = stridor at rest, mild recession: oral dexamethasone, observe 4 hours.
Severe = marked recession, agitation or hypoxia: dexamethasone + nebulised adrenaline.
Impending failure = silent chest, exhaustion, cyanosis: anaesthetic intubation.
Avoid examining the throat in suspected epiglottitis; consider if drooling, toxic appearance.
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone oral liquid is bitter; mixing with a small amount of flavoured ice block or juice improves uptake. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg is a reasonable substitute if dexamethasone is unavailable but is shorter-acting and has higher relapse rates.

stem: A previously well 7-week-old female infant is brought to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of fever and poor feeding. She had a normal antenatal and postnatal course. Her parents report no rash, no respiratory symptoms and no obvious source of infection. She has been breastfeeding but feeds have shortened to about 5 minutes per side.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 52, SpO2 99% RA, T 38.6°C, capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Pale but well perfused, soft fontanelle, chest clear, abdomen soft, no rash, no focal source identified
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine sample: leukocytes 3+, nitrites positive, dipstick blood trace; urine culture pending; FBC/UEC/CRP sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge on oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily with GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Infants under 3 months with febrile UTI require admission and parenteral antibiotics until culture and clinical stability allow oral switch. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Admit for IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and complete septic workup including LP
options.rationale: Under 3 months with fever and possible UTI requires full septic workup (blood and urine cultures, lumbar puncture) and broad IV cover with benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin until cultures confirm a source. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile young infant)

options.text: Commence oral trimethoprim 4 mg/kg twice daily for 3 days and arrange renal ultrasound
options.rationale: Short oral trimethoprim courses are inappropriate for febrile UTI in infants under 3 months and miss other potential sources of sepsis. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Repeat urine collection by clean catch and observe in short stay if afebrile
options.rationale: A catheter sample with leucocytes and nitrites is sufficient for working UTI in this age group; delaying treatment increases risk of bacteraemia or meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Arrange outpatient micturating cystourethrogram before considering antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Imaging is part of post-treatment workup in proven first UTI under 6 months but does not delay empirical antibiotics. (Source: NICE NG224)
source_refs: NICE NG224 urinary tract infection in under 16s

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 3 months are at substantial risk of serious bacterial infection. A positive catheter urine specimen still mandates full septic workup including lumbar puncture and broad empirical IV antibiotics (benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin per RCH and Queensland). Oral therapy is started after 48 hours of stability with confirmed urinary source and clinical improvement. Imaging (renal ultrasound) is arranged during admission; MCUG is considered for recurrent UTI, atypical organisms or abnormal ultrasound.
key_points: Under 3 months febrile UTI = admit, full septic workup including LP, IV antibiotics.
Empirical regimen: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin; cefotaxime if meningitis confirmed.
Switch to oral after 48 hours if stable and culture-sensitive.
Renal ultrasound within 6 weeks for all first UTI under 6 months.
Continue prophylactic trimethoprim only if VUR grade 3 or more confirmed.
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin once-daily dosing (7 mg/kg term, 5 mg/kg preterm) reduces nephrotoxicity risk; check trough at 48 hours before the third dose to confirm clearance.

sources.label: NICE NG224 urinary tract infection in under 16s

stem: A 14-month-old boy attends his GP for a routine review. His parents are not concerned but the practice nurse noted pallor at the immunisation visit. He drinks around 800 mL of cow's milk daily from a bottle, eats few solid foods and has had three brief ED visits for transient pallor. He is developmentally normal and growing along the 25th centile.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, soft systolic flow murmur, abdomen soft, no organomegaly, no bruising
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86 g/L, MCV 64 fL, ferritin 6 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 8 percent, reticulocyte count 0.6 percent, CRP 2 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start a 3-month trial of pyridoxine and check haemoglobin electrophoresis urgently
options.rationale: Pyridoxine targets sideroblastic anaemia; electrophoresis is for thalassaemia or haemoglobinopathy and is not first-line in classical dietary iron deficiency. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Arrange intravenous ferric carboxymaltose infusion in the day-procedure unit instead
options.rationale: IV iron is reserved for malabsorption, intolerance to oral iron, or failure after a documented trial; first presentation deserves an oral trial. (Source: AMH: ferric carboxymaltose)

options.text: Refer urgently to haematology for bone marrow aspiration and trephine biopsy soonest
options.rationale: Pancytopenia, blasts, or unexplained iron deficiency unresponsive to therapy warrant marrow studies; not first-line for typical dietary iron deficiency. (Source: HSANZ paediatric anaemia)

options.text: Transfuse one unit of packed red blood cells and admit for haemodynamic monitoring
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia (Hb under 70 g/L, decompensation); this child is stable. (Source: NBA patient blood management)

options.text: Commence oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg elemental iron daily and reduce milk intake
options.rationale: Microcytic anaemia with low ferritin and excessive cow's milk is classic toddler iron deficiency; AU first-line therapy is oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg elemental iron daily with dietary counselling. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency is the commonest paediatric anaemia in Australia, peaking from 9 to 24 months when excessive cow's milk displaces iron-rich solids. Microcytic anaemia with ferritin under 12 mcg/L confirms iron deficiency. AU first-line is oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg daily for 3 months after Hb normalises (to replenish stores), plus reducing cow's milk to under 500 mL daily and increasing iron-rich foods. IV iron is reserved for intolerance, malabsorption or failure of oral therapy.
key_points: Risk factors: prematurity, excessive cow's milk over 500 mL daily, vegetarian or restricted diet, low birth weight.
Investigations: FBC + MCV, ferritin (acute phase reactant), transferrin saturation, CRP to exclude infection-driven changes.
First-line: oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg elemental iron daily for 3 to 6 months.
Reduce cow's milk to under 500 mL daily; encourage red meat, eggs, fortified cereals.
Recheck FBC and ferritin at 4 weeks; expect Hb rise of 10 g/L within 1 month.
explanation.pearls: Use ferrous sulfate liquid 30 mg per mL elemental iron; give with vitamin C-containing juice and at least 2 hours apart from milk to optimise absorption.

subtopic_detail: Acute moderate asthma in school-age child
stem: A 7-year-old boy with a known history of asthma is brought to the emergency department by his mother with a 12-hour history of increasing wheeze and cough triggered by a viral illness. He is using his salbutamol puffer with a spacer every 2 hours at home without sustained relief. He has no peanut or food allergies. His last admission for asthma was 12 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, RR 30, SpO2 93% RA, T 37.4°C, peak flow 60 percent predicted
data_table.Examination: Mild accessory muscle use, audible expiratory wheeze bilaterally, speaks in short phrases, alert and cooperative
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated initially; CXR reserved for atypical features

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses, then reassess clinically
options.rationale: Pressurised metered-dose inhaler with spacer is at least as effective as nebuliser in children and avoids unnecessary aerosolisation; nebuliser is reserved for severe disease. (Source: National Asthma Council: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

options.text: Commence high flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min and refer to PICU
options.rationale: HFNC is reserved for severe or life-threatening asthma with persistent hypoxia; this presentation is moderate. (Source: ANZICS guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg in normal saline over 20 minutes
options.rationale: IV magnesium is reserved for severe asthma not responding to initial salbutamol burst therapy; first-line is salbutamol plus steroids. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

options.text: Give salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for 1 hour and oral prednisolone
options.rationale: Moderate exacerbation (SpO2 90 to 94 percent, accessory muscle use, peak flow 50 to 75 percent) is managed with salbutamol via spacer (6 puffs under 6 years, 12 puffs over 6 years - but 6 puffs is standard AU paediatric dose) every 20 minutes for 1 hour plus oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg (max 50 mg). (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

options.text: Increase his inhaled corticosteroid maintenance dose and discharge with parent action plan
options.rationale: Maintenance ICS escalation is for ongoing control; acute exacerbation requires bronchodilator burst plus oral steroids. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook 2024)

explanation.summary: Moderate asthma exacerbation in school-age children is treated with salbutamol via pressurised metered-dose inhaler and spacer (12 puffs over 6 years, 6 puffs under 6 years) every 20 minutes for the first hour, plus a single dose of oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg up to 50 mg, with reassessment after each dose. Spacer delivery is at least as effective as nebuliser, avoids aerosolisation, and reduces emergency drug usage. Severe disease (SpO2 under 90, silent chest, exhaustion) escalates to nebulised salbutamol with ipratropium and IV magnesium.
key_points: Mild = SpO2 over 94, mild wheeze, normal speech: salbutamol 4 to 6 puffs, observe.
Moderate = SpO2 90 to 94, accessory muscle use: salbutamol burst + oral pred.
Severe = silent chest, agitated, hypoxia: nebulised salbutamol-ipratropium, IV magnesium, ICU.
Discharge: stable for 1 to 2 hours, SpO2 over 94, salbutamol 3-hourly or less; action plan; oral pred 3 days.
Refer all severe exacerbations to paediatric review within 1 week.
explanation.pearls: For children with frequent exacerbations or poor symptom control on SABA, consider starting low-dose ICS (budesonide 200 mcg twice daily) and the AsthmaSCAN action plan for written symptom tracking.
